titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the David Slavitt Papers
titlestmt.author: by Emma Gronbeck

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday March 1, 2023 at 04:12

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection contains writings, printed material, photographs, personal and professional papers, and memorabilia created and collected by American author, poet, and translator David Slavitt. Throughout his career, Slavitt published works of poetry, erotic novels, memoirs, and thrillers, as well as English translations of works from Latin, Greek, Hebrew, Italian, Spanish, French, Old English, and Sanskrit. Also included are correspondence and writings of Slavitt's family, friends, and colleagues, including Fred Chappell, Kelly Cherry, Greg Delanty, Richard H. W. Dillard, Richard Elman, Brendan Galvin, George Garrett, Dana Gioia, Rachel Hadas, Adrien Stoutenburg, and Henry Taylor.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 94 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Access copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 95 (audiovisual media): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of David R. Slavitt, 2001, and purchased from David R. Slavitt on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2015.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1950-2011. II. Writings, 1950-2013. III. Personal and Professional Papers, 1948-2013. IV. Printed Material, 1946-2014. V. Computer and Audiovisual Media, circa 1967-1990s.

bioghist.head: David R. Slavitt (1935-)
bioghist.p: David Rytman Slavitt is a Jewish-American writer, editor, and translator. Born in White Plains, New York on March 23, 1935, Slavitt earned his B.A., magna cum laude , from Yale University in 1956, where he was a member of the "Scholars of the House" program as class poet. He completed his M.A. in English at Columbia University in New York City in 1957. Slavitt is primarily known for his collections of poetry, including Cheater's Dozen: Eleven Poems , which he self-published in 1952, Suits for the Dead (1961), Dozens (1981), PS3569.L3 (1998), and Falling from Silence: Poems (2001). Slavitt has published over 100 novels, poetry anthologies, nonfiction books, and translations over the course of his literary career, at times using pseudonyms such as Henry Sutton, Lynn Meyer (his first wife's name), Henry Lazarus, and David Benjamin. Novels include The Carnivore (1965), Anagrams (1970), The Proposal (1980), The Hussar (1987), and Salazar Blinks (1988).

After publishing his first work of translation Eclogues of Virgil , in 1971, Slavitt increasingly focused on translating classical Greek and Latin poetry and plays, as well as other works in French, Italian, Hebrew, and Sanskrit. Translated works include The Metamorphoses of Ovid (1994), Sonnets of Love and Death of Jean de Sponde (2001), Propertius in Love: The Elegies (2002), Orlando Furioso: a new verse translation (2009), The Metabolism of Desire: The Poems of Guido Cavalcanti (2012), and The Mahabharata (2015). Slavitt has also taught literature and writing workshops at Temple University, Columbia University, the University of Pennsylvania, Princeton University, and Bennington College.
As of 2022, Slavitt and his second wife, Janet Lee Abrahm, a professor at Harvard Medical School, live in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: David Slavitt Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing. This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains writings, printed material, photographs, personal and professional papers, and memorabilia created and collected by American author, poet, and translator David Slavitt. Throughout his career, Slavitt published works of poetry, erotic novels, memoirs, and thrillers, as well as English translations of works from Latin, Greek, Hebrew, Italian, Spanish, French, Old English, and Sanskrit. Also included are correspondence and writings of Slavitt's family, friends, and colleagues, including Fred Chappell, Kelly Cherry, Greg Delanty, Richard H. W. Dillard, Richard Elman, Brendan Galvin, George Garrett, Dana Gioia, Rachel Hadas, Adrien Stoutenburg, and Henry Taylor.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Serials in the collection were indexed and discarded. A bibliography of these materials can be found in Appendix A.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The David Slavitt Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

persname.part: Dillard, R. H. W. (Richard H. W.), 1937-

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of correspondence of David Slavitt and his family, friends, and professional contacts. Many letters are enclosed with notes, writings, and clippings. Correspondents include fellow poets and writers George Garrett, Aubrey Goodman, Rachel Hadas, and Adrien Stoutenburg; publishers Doubleday, Louisiana State University Press, and University of North Carolina Press; and Slavitt's first wife, Lynn Meyer Slavitt, his sister, Susan Slavitt, and his parents, Samuel and Adele Slavitt.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Alphabetical Correspondence, and Chronological Correspondence.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Correspondence can also be found in Series II. Writings and Series III. Personal and Professional Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of drafts and proofs of Slavitt's poetry, prose, and translations, as well as notes, related correspondence, contracts, and publicity materials. Also included are writings and translations of other authors, such as Kelly Cherry, Greg Delanty, Richard H. W. Dillard, Richard Elman, Brendan Galvin, George Garrett, Dana Gioia, Rachel Hadas, Mark Rudman, and Henry Taylor.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Poetry and Prose, and Translations.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Writings can also be found in Series I. Correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes drafts, proofs, correspondence, and other records related to Slavitt's short and long-form poems, novels, non-fiction books, essays, plays, screenplays, book and film reviews, memoirs, and short fiction. Drafts of early works of erotic fiction, such as The Exhibitionist (1967) and The Voyeur (1968), which Slavitt published under the pseudonym Henry Sutton, are included, as well as novels such as The Hussar (1987) and The Cliff (1994), and poetry anthologies such as Vital Signs (1975) and The Seven Deadly Sins and Other Poems (2009).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by title, with untitled and unsorted materials and writings of others filed at the end of the sequence.

"A Nuptial Cento Modeled on that Decius Magnus Ausonius"

The Seven Deadly Sins and Other Poems

did.unittitle: "Tuppingware, or What Are You Doing After the Orgy?"

title.part: Understanding Social Life: An Introduction to Social Psychology

did.unittitle: "The View from the Room at the Top"

title.part: Vital Signs: New and Selected Poems
Vital Signs: New and Selected Poems
William Henry Harrison and Other Poems
The Woods Are Full of Naked Ladies

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains drafts, proofs, correspondence, and other records related to Slavitt's works of translation and collections of translated works edited by Slavitt. Works of translation include the poetry and plays of Roman authors Virgil, Ovid, Seneca, Avianus, and Propertius, and Greek authors Aeschylus and Sophocles. Also included are works translated from Spanish, French, Italian, Hebrew, Old English, and Sanskrit, such as The poem of Queen Esther by João Pinto Delgado (1999), Sonnets of Love and Death of Jean de Sponde (2001), Orlando Furioso: a new verse translation (2009), Sixty-One Psalms of David (1996), The Battle of Maldon (2013) and The Mahabharata (2015).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by title, with unsorted translations and translations of others filed at the end of the sequence.

title.part: The Book of Lamentations: a Meditation and Translation
The Book of the Twelve Prophets
Broken Columns: Two Roman Epic Fragments
Commemorations of the Professors of Bordeaux
The Crooning Wind: Three Greenlandic Poets
The Eclogues and the Georgics of Virgil
The Eclogues and the Georgics of Virgil
Epic and Epigram: Two Elizabethan Entertainments
Epinician Odes and Dithyrambs of Bacchylides
The Gnat and Other Poems of the Appendix Virgiliana
Hymns of Prudentius: The Cathemerinon, or, The Daily Round
The Latin Odes of Jean Dorat
The Lays of Marie de France
Love Poems, Letters, and Remedies of Ovid
The Metabolism of Desire: The Poems of Guido Cavalcanti
Orlando Furioso: a new verse translation
Orlando Furioso: a new verse translation
Orlando Furioso: a new verse translation
The Other Four Plays of Sophocles: Ajax, Women of Trachis, Electra, and Philoctetes
The Poem of Queen Esther by João Pinto Delgado
Procne by Gregorio Correr; a new translation by David R. Slavitt
The Regrets of Joachim du Bellay
Solomon Ibn Gabirol's A Crown For the King
Sonnets of Love and Death of Jean de Sponde
Sonnets of Love and Death of Jean de Sponde
The Voyage of the Argo: The Argonautica of Gaius Valerius Flaccus
The Voyage of the Argo: The Argonautica of Gaius Valerius Flaccus

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains financial records, royalty statements, photographs, legal and real estate records, publicity and promotional materials, scrapbooks, and David Slavitt's school papers and writings from Phillips Academy, Yale University, and Columbia University. Also included are personal and family memorabilia, promotional items from book publications, papers of Slavitt's children, calendars, and teaching materials.

did.unittitle: Yale Scholar of the House program

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains newspaper clippings, reviews of David Slavitt's work, theater and event programs, publication catalogs, and unsorted ephemera.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Clippings and Ephemera, and Publishing Catalogs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes newspaper clippings and photocopies of articles on topics of interest to David Slavitt, reviews of his work, and printed ephemera, such as flyers, souvenirs, maps, event programs, and playbills.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes catalogs from presses and booksellers that printed or distributed Slavitt's work, such as John Hopkins University Press and Louisiana State University Press.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by title, with unsorted catalogs filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains computer and audiovisual media, including reels of ½-inch audio tape, 5-inch floppy disks, and one 3 1/2-inch floppy disk containing drafts of David Slavitt's writing and writings of others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Computer Media, and Audiovisual Media.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 94 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Access copies of electronic files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 95 (audiovisual media): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains partial audio recordings of Slavitt's novels The Exhibitionist , Vector , and The Voyeur .
